text.title: Solo Rate Floor Examples
text.summary: Worked examples — one defensible floor, one set of inputs the methodology rejects.

example.title: $120k US solo, baseline
example.note: G=$120000, m=0.20, t=0.34 (US incl. SE tax), B=$8000 (private health + retirement), T=$3000 (tools), W=46, p=0.55. needed_revenue = (120000+8000+3000)*(1.20)/(0.66) = 238182. billable_hours = 46*40*0.55 = 1012. floor = $235/hr nominal; rounded $235. Wait — recheck: 238182/1012 ≈ 235. Sensitivity: p=0.45 → 286/hr; p=0.65 → 199/hr. Defence narrative: "Loaded rate accounts for self-employment tax + private benefits + 20% reinvestment margin standard for senior independent practice. The number below is 35% below median Bay Area senior contractor rates." floor: 235. project_minimum_usd: 4700. validator: PASS.

example.title: The naive 'spreadsheet floor' the methodology rejects
example.note: G=$120000, naive operator divides by 52*40=2080 → $58/hr. Inputs to this methodology: t=0.10 (forgot SE tax), W=52, p=0.80, m=0.05, B=0 (forgot health). The validator rejects: t too low (jurisdictional floor 0.32), W > 46, p > 0.55 without data, m < 0.15, B should be ≥ jurisdictional minimum. Halt: cannot produce a defensible floor on these inputs. Operator must re-collect inputs with realistic numbers; the spreadsheet $58/hr is the canonical "wage rate, not business rate" trap.
